event_name: UFC Fight Night: Song vs. Figueiredo

fights.fighter_a_bio: Song Yadong hunts for knockouts with heavy hands and aggressive forward pressure, but he loads up on strikes that leave him vulnerable to counters. His tendency to eat shots while chasing finishes is exactly what an elite grappler with Figueiredo's control game targets.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Deiveson Figueiredo is a submission specialist who controls pace through relentless takedowns and suffocating top position—he grinds opponents into fatigue rather than flash. Our model gives him 80.2% to win because Song's wild striking angles are tailor-made for someone who locks in positional control.
fights.watch_for: Does Song's chin hold up early? If Figueiredo lands a clean takedown in rounds 1-2 and establishes top control, Song's cardio and defense typically collapse under sustained pressure, turning this into a grappling clinic.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Zhang Mingyang is a volume striker who wins by outlanding opponents with combinations and maintaining distance with kicks. His Elo (1531.5) is lower than Menifield's, but our model gives him 92.6% to win because his striking output typically overwhelms opponents who can't match his pace.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Alonzo Menifield has a power wrestling base and can explode into takedowns, but he's a 188 underdog here. Vegas and our model both heavily favor Zhang, seeing Menifield's grappling as a long shot against a striker this active.
fights.watch_for: Can Menifield land a takedown and control Zhang on the ground, or does Zhang's constant striking volume force Menifield to eat damage while hunting for that shot?

fights.fighter_a_bio: Pavlovich is a heavyweight sledgehammer who overwhelms opponents with brutal striking volume and relentless forward pressure, mixing heavy hands with clinch work to grind out wins. Our model favors him at 65%, and Vegas backs that hard (-509 odds) — he's the more proven fighter here with a 1587 Elo rating.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Teixeira is a pure striker who lives on the feet and needs space to land his combinations; he lacks the grappling to threaten Pavlovich on the ground and will struggle if pushed against the cage. At +372 odds and 1504 Elo, he's a genuine underdog with a real path only if he can avoid the clinch.
fights.watch_for: Whether Teixeira can stay on the outside and use footwork to pick Pavlovich apart before eating a clinch or getting backed into the fence — that's where Pavlovich's pressure wrestling becomes a problem.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Kai Asakura uses speed and footwork to create angles, then attacks with volume—he'll throw combinations early and often to break down opponents. The model gives him 65.7% to win, and Vegas agrees heavily (-307), likely because his pace and aggression give him consistent paths to victory.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Smotherman relies on counter-striking and patience, waiting for openings before landing hard shots rather than forcing the action. He's the underdog here (246 moneyline), which suggests he'll need to make Asakura miss and capitalize on mistakes rather than dictate the fight.
fights.watch_for: Can Smotherman stay composed early when Asakura's combinations are landing, or will the pace force him to brawl and lose the counter-striking advantage that's his path to an upset?

fights.fighter_a_bio: Salikhov hunts with precise striking—he's patient, picks shots, and uses footwork to slide out of danger. He controls distance with kicks and exploits openings with clean combinations.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Matthews fights aggressively on the front foot and isn't afraid to trade strikes or crash through the pocket. He's tougher to break down when he's moving forward and pressuring, but lapses into defensive scrambling when he can't dictate the pace.
fights.watch_for: Can Matthews sustain forward pressure for 15 minutes, or will Salikhov's timing and footwork create enough space to pick him apart?

fights.fighter_a_bio: Perez leans on his wrestling to drag fights to the mat where he controls pace with top pressure and ground-and-pound. Vegas likes him here—his Elo rating and our model both back him as the safer pick at -139.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Sumudaerji hunts for striking exchanges and isn't afraid to trade, but that aggression can leave openings against someone comfortable wrestling him down. At +117, he's the underdog precisely because Perez has the grappling tools to shut down his approach.
fights.watch_for: Whether Sumudaerji can keep the fight standing and make Perez respect his hands early—if Perez gets the clinch and drags him down, the wrestling control becomes the fight.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Yi Sak Lee comes in as an even 50-50 proposition according to our model, despite Vegas giving him underdog odds at +137. We don't have detailed fight film on his tendencies yet, so this is a toss-up built on limited data.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Luis Felipe Dias is Vegas's pick at -166, favored over Lee despite identical 50% win probability from our model. Like his opponent, we're working with incomplete information on his actual fighting patterns.
fights.watch_for: Early aggression and cage control will likely separate these two—whoever dictates the pace and prevents being pushed backward in the first round probably wins, since we can't predict their specific technical matchup.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Ding Meng sits at an even 50-50 with our model, suggesting a true coin flip matchup. At 1500 Elo with moderate unpredictability, he's an unproven fighter who could surprise or struggle depending on the night.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Jose Henrique mirrors Ding Meng's profile—dead even odds, identical Elo rating, and the same moderate chaos index. This is a pick-em fight between two fighters with limited track record to separate them.
fights.watch_for: Watch whether either fighter can establish control early; in a matchup this even with limited history on both, whoever dictates pace and position in round one likely wins the decision.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Aoriqileng lands hard strikes and controls distance with kicks—he picks opponents apart from range rather than brawling. Our model sees him as a heavy favorite (92.8%) because his timing and output have proven reliable against comparable competition.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Haddon has the slightly higher Elo rating and is Vegas's pick, but he hasn't shown the same striking precision or fight IQ that Aoriqileng consistently displays. He'll need to create chaos early or clinch to negate the reach disadvantage.
fights.watch_for: Whether Haddon can close distance and drag this into a phone booth fight in rounds 2–3, or if Aoriqileng maintains range and racks up striking volume for a comfortable decision.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Rei Tsuruya is a submission threat who hunts for takedowns and controls opponents on the mat—he's not looking for a dance-off, he's looking for a tap. Vegas and our model agree he's the pick at -340, backed by his 1501.8 Elo and a grappling approach that limits how messy fights get.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Jesus Aguilar strikes and grapples but doesn't dominate either—he's reactive rather than aggressive with his gameplan. At +267, he's the underdog despite a solid 1514.5 Elo, suggesting Vegas thinks Tsuruya's mat control will smother his attempts to stay on his feet.
fights.watch_for: Whether Aguilar can stuff Tsuruya's takedowns early and make him respect his striking; if Tsuruya lands even one clean double-leg in rounds 1-2, expect him to ride out the rest on top.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Angela Hill throws volume from the outside and uses her reach to control distance with jabs and leg kicks. Our model gives her a slight edge (50.3%) in what's essentially a coin flip between two fighters with nearly identical records.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Xiong Jingnan attacks aggressively and looks to close distance, pressuring opponents into the fence where she can work clinch control and knees. She's proven at this level but faces a fighter whose output and range make her difficult to pin down.
fights.watch_for: Whether Xiong can close the distance and trap Hill against the cage for sustained clinch work, or if Hill keeps her at range with footwork and kicks for five rounds.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Zhu Kangjie enters this fight as a slight favorite according to our model (50.1% win probability), though Vegas hasn't posted clear odds yet. Both fighters sit at nearly identical Elo ratings, suggesting this is a toss-up on paper.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Ramon Taveras matches Zhu's credentials almost exactly—same Elo rating, same chaos index, and our model gives him a near-50/50 shot. Without detailed fight footage available, this is a true coin flip.
fights.watch_for: Early aggression and control will be the differentiator here. Whoever establishes their pace in round one and dictates where the fight happens—whether that's on the feet or the mat—likely takes the decision on a night with minimal track record to lean on.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Loma Lookboonmee relies on footwork and timing to land strikes from range, then clinches to grind opponents against the cage. Vegas loves her at -136, but her 1566.6 Elo suggests she hasn't faced elite competition recently.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Jaqueline Amorim hunts takedowns early and uses top control to wear opponents down—she's not looking for flashy finishes, just dominance. Our model gives her 92.3% to win, and her pressure-heavy style matches up badly for Lookboonmee's range game.
fights.watch_for: Whether Lookboonmee can stay off her back and maintain distance for three rounds, or if Amorim secures even one takedown early and rides top position to a decision.
